cit.q: You … touch the palm-leaf [thatching] our cookhouse, as the big elephant bull going round for alms …, [and] stand [there]!

That dark young woman, she [is] in the cookhouse, being vexed with us, she who wiped off with the top of [her] pretty cloth the perspiration in many minute [drops], impressed on her crescent forehead befittingly(?), she with eyes at strife with the touch of smoke ...

cit.ref: Ciṟupāṇāṟṟuppaṭai lines 130–132 (trans. Visweswaran 2026, altered by Ferenczi)
cit.q: tiṟavāk kaṇṇa cāy cevik kuruḷai / kaṟavāp pāl mulai kavartal nōṉātu / puṉiṟṟu nāy kuraikkum pulleṉ aṭṭil
… in the empty cookhouse, where a recently whelped dog barks, unable to endure the grasping of her milky teats not [yet] milked by [her] puppies with unopened eyes and curved ears ….

the smoke from cookhouses, the smoke [from] dumplings of pancake sellers that don’t dash against [each other [in] the wide bazaar, husbands [and their] women that lifted in their storeyed houses pretty, sweet smoke, and the smoke from sacrificial fires, the many, different flower[-like] smoke
[Her] brilliant face sweat. [Her already] red eyes [further] reddened. As she who watched the cookhouse that is charred on the outside ...
aruḷ puri neñcattu aṟavōr paḷḷiyum / aṭṭil cālaiyum aruntunar cālaiyum
… the alms-house/choultry (paḷḷi) of gracious-hearted virtuous men/of the Virtuous Man (Buddha?) of graceful heart, with a hall [serving as] cookhouse and hall for those who dine …
ciṟukālai / aṭṭil pukātāḷ arum piṇi
She who does not enter the cookhouse at dawn is a rare disease [to her husband].
uraṟkaḷattum aṭṭilum peṇṭirkaḷ mēlum / naṭukkaṟṟa kāṭciyār nōkkār eṭutticaiyār / illam pukāar viṭal

sense.note: Etymology: aṭu (‘to cook’, r.n. obl.) + il (‘house’, n.).

This meaning is a transparent rendering of the term's etymology, designating a structure used for cooking that is typically separated or structurally distinct from the main living quarters, its detachment motivated primarily by the hazards of smoke and fire (cf. Cilappatikāram 13: 122; 16: 32; also cf. aṭukkaḷai in Ācārakkōvai 39: 2; Maṇimēkalai 29: 61).

Although 20. 7–8 describes a 'choultry' or 'refectory' as a whole, this attestation is assigned to the present meaning because the author distinguishes between the 'refectory kitchen' and the 'dining hall' within the same location; the term here thus designates the former rather than the latter.
Tivākaram 960: aṭṭil aṭukkaḷai maṭaippaḷḷi ākum. — “aṭṭil is a cookhouse and a choultry/refectory.”
Piṅkalam 692: aṭṭil maṭaippaḷḷi aṭukkaḷai ākum. — “aṭṭil is a choultry/refectory and a cookhouse.” (Trans. Ferenczi)

cit.q: … on the side of the big mountain, where waterfalls roar, sounds rising as if from the choultry of Utiyaṉ whose heart does not shy away from what is said [to be] the duty of giving …
pulip poṟip pōrk kataviṉ / tirut tuñcum tiṇ kāppiṉ / pukaḻ nilaiiya moḻi viḷaṅka / aṟaṉ nilaiiya akaṉ aṭṭil / cōṟu vākkiya koḻum kañci

the thick rice-water, drained from the cooked rice, [flowing] from a large choultry (aṭṭil) where the duty (aṟam) was constant, when words (moḻi) became abundant and praises (pukaḻ) were permanent, [behind] the firm protection [of ramparts] where fortune rests, [and behind] the gates covered with the Tiger Mark …
koṭum kāl māṭattu neṭum kaṭai tuvaṉṟi / virunt’ uṇṭ’ āṉāp perum cōṟṟ’ aṭṭil / oḷ cuvar nal il uyar tiṇai ...

… [staying] in the high verandah of the fine house with shining walls [and] a choultry where the Great Rice (peruñcōṟu) is never exhausted, even after being eaten by guests, after [they] gathered at the tall gates of the storied buildings with curved pillars …

sense.note: In a royal or institutional context, the term designates a dedicated facility where meals are prepared and served (cf. maṭaippaḷḷi), functioning as a choultry or refectory for poets, guests, pilgrims, and dependants.

"Choultry, s. Peculiar to S. India, and of doubtful etymology; Malayāl. chāwaṭī, Tel. chāwaḍi, [tsāvaḍi, chau, Skt. chatur, 'four,' vāṭa, 'road, a place where four roads meet]. In W. India the form used is chowry or chowree (Dakh. chāoṛī). A hall, a shed, or a simple loggia, used by travellers as a resting-place, and also intended for the transaction of public business. [...]" (Hobson-Jobson 1903, p. 212).

cit.q: pulip poṟip pōrk kataviṉ / tirut tuñcum tiṇ kāppiṉ / pukaḻ nilaiiya moḻi viḷaṅka / aṟaṉ nilaiiya akaṉ aṭṭil / cōṟu vākkiya koḻum kañci

the thick rice-water, drained from the cooked rice, [flowing] from a large Cookhouse (aṭṭil) where the Virtue/Dharma (aṟam) was constant, when spells (moḻi) became abundant and hymns (pukaḻ) were permanent, [behind] the firm protection [of ramparts] where Tiru (Śrī) rests, [and behind] the gates covered with the Tiger Mark …

... the seat of the ones with the auspicious Vedas, who appeared as glorious as mountains encircled by clouds, having covered the very high storied mansions everywhere with the fragrant smoke of the āvuti created by [those] Vedic people [in] the Cookhouse that glitters with fire, that gives life to the offspring of the rain clouds, also to the victory of the strong man with a deerskin [cloak?], tiger [emblem], and a chariot with banners ...

sense.note: In a semantic shift, the same term comes to denote the site of Vedic fire sacrifice (cf. ōmacālai).
Paṭṭiṉappālai lines 40–44 recur here, as the passage lends itself to a cilēṭai/śleṣa encompassing both a refectory and a sacrificial hall.

bibl.title: A comprehensive Tamil and English dictionary (Winslow)

cit.q: aṭukkaḷai
cookhouse
kariya iṭattēyuṭaiya aṭṭil
cookhouse that is charred on the outside …
aṭukkaḷai
cookhouse
immaikkup pukaḻ nilaipeṟṟa col eṅkum paravā niṟka maṟumaikku aṟam nilaipeṟṟa akaṉṟa aṭṭil, irumaiyiṉum peṟum payaṉ uṇṭākac cōṟ’ iṭum cālai eṉṟavāṟu.

There is a way to say that the wide ‘cookhouse’, which stands firm in virtue for the next life, while words of praise for this life, being permanent, continue to spread everywhere, [that ‘cookhouse’ is] a house (cālai), where cooked rice is distributed abundantly, which [yields] great benefit for both [this life and the next]
aṭukkaḷai
cookhouse
maḻaikkum karuppam āvatu tūmam. aṭṭil – ōmacālai.
The smoke has become the womb even for the rain clouds. aṭṭil – sacrificial hall (ōmacālai from Skt. homaśālā)
... maḻaikkum karuppattait tōṟṟuvikkum aḻalai viḷakkukiṉṟa cālaiyiṉ uṇṭāṉa maṟaiyōrāṉ ākkappaṭṭa tēvaruṇāviṉ āvi ...
... the fragrant smoke of the divine food, made by the Vedic people to be eaten [by the fire], in the hall that kindles the fire that brings forth the offspring of the rain clouds …

listBibl.bibl: Puṣparataceṭṭiyār, Ū., ed. 1885. Nālaṭiyār eṉṟu vaḻaṅkum nālaṭi nāṉūṟu mūlamum atarkut teḷiporuḷ viḷakkap patavuraiyum. Ceṉṉapaṭṭaṉam Kalāratnānakaram eṉṉum accukkūṭam. ark:/13960/s2cw8d42tsb

Nārāyaṇacaraṇar, ed. 1899. Ācārakkōvai. Maturaiyil caṅkam aruviya cāṉṟōril oruvar ākiya kayattūr peruvāyiṉ muḷḷiyāciriyarāl iyaṟṟiya mūlamum, nacciṉārkkiṉiyārāṟ ceyta poḻippuraiyum. Tañcāvūr: Śrī kiruṣṇavilāca acciyantiracālai. ark:/13960/t0xq4g53d
Cāminātaiyar, U. Vē., ed. 1889. Pattuppāṭṭu mūlamum maturaiyāciriyar pārattuvāci nacciṉārkkiṉiyar uraiyum. 1st edition. Ceṉṉai: Tirāviṭaratnākara Accukkūṭam. ark:/13960/s2jdk2wm5hp
Cāminātaiyar, U. Vē., ed. 1955. Cilappatikāra mūlamum. arumpatavuraiyum. Aṭiyārkkunallāruraiyum. Ceṉṉai: Kapīr Accukkūṭam. ark:/13960/s2x53j5nnx3

Yule, Henry, Sir. 1903. Hobson-Jobson: A glossary of colloquial Anglo-Indian words and phrases, and of kindred terms, etymological, historical, geographical and discursive. New ed. edited by William Crooke, B.A. London: J. Murray.
Cāminātaiyar, U. Vē., ed. 1898. Maṇimēkalai mūlamum, Vē. Cāminātaiyareḻutiya arumpatavuraiyum. Ceṉṉai: Ve. Nā. Jūpili Accukkūṭam. ark:/13960/s27s9xkc69s
